perturbation.original_text: At the request of the Disclosing Party, the Recipient shall promptly return to the Disclosing Party all Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party in whatever medium, including any and all copies thereof. Such return (or, if authorized, destruction of Confidential Information) shall be confirmed in writing by the Recipient to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: At the request of the Disclosing Party, the Recipient shall return the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information within 90 days of the request. Such return (or, if authorized, destruction of Confidential Information) shall be confirmed orally by the Recipient to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the return timeframe to 90 days and confirmation to oral introduces non-compliance with standard business practices and could violate Pennsylvania's implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_citation: Pennsylvania law recognizes the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing in contracts.
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania's implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing requires parties to act reasonably and honestly in performing their contractual duties. A 90-day return period for confidential information and oral confirmation is unreasonable and could be interpreted as a breach of this covenant, especially if the information is time-sensitive.

Code , and Canadian jurisprudence in both Québec and the common law provinces pertaining to contractual and pre-contractual negotiation v t e In contract law , the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing is a general presumption that the parties to a contract will deal with each other honestly, fairly, and in good faith , so as to not destroy the right of the other party or parties to receive the benefits of the contract. It is implied in a number of contract types in order to reinforce the express covenants or promises of the contract. A lawsuit (or a cause of action ) based upon the breach of the covenant may arise when one party to the contract attempts to claim the benefit of a technical excuse for breaching the contract, or when he or she uses specific contractual terms in isolation in order to refuse to perform his or her contractual obligations, despite the general circumstances and understandings between the parties. When a court or trier of fact interprets a contract, there is always an "implied covenant of good fai
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a 90-day return period for confidential information and allows oral confirmation of its return or destruction, which contradicts the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under Pennsylvania law. The original text mandates prompt return and written confirmation. The law explanation supports that the 90-day return period could be unreasonable, especially if the information is time-sensitive. The 'Implied covenant of honesty and fair dealing' sourced from Wikipedia states that this covenant is 'a general presumption that the parties to a contract will deal with each other honestly, fairly, and in good faith, so as to not destroy the right of the other party or parties to receive the benefits of the contract.' Allowing a 90-day delay and accepting oral confirmation weakens the protection of confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by an interpreted in accordance with the laws of the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania, without giving effect to any conflict of law provisions.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and interpreted in accordance with the laws of the People's Republic of China, without giving effect to any conflict of law provisions.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to the People's Republic of China is against the clause of choice of law, where the contract was originally between two corporations in the USA. Pennsylvania law would be more appropriate.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract - Choice of Law
perturbation.law_citation: Principles of contract law related to choice of law and enforceability.
perturbation.law_explanation: While parties can generally choose the governing law, a US court may refuse to enforce a choice-of-law clause if it violates public policy or bears no reasonable relationship to the contract. Choosing Chinese law for a contract between two US companies operating in the US is unlikely to be upheld.

perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies Pennsylvania law. The changed text specifies the law of the People's Republic of China. The Law Explanation suggests that choosing Chinese law for a contract between two US companies operating in the US is unlikely to be upheld, because a US court may refuse to enforce a choice-of-law clause if it violates public policy or bears no reasonable relationship to the contract. This creates a contradiction because the altered text introduces a governing law that may not be legally sound for the parties involved. This falls under the definition of Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction as the change makes contractual terms unenforceable or illegal.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient shall take responsible steps to ensure that all Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party is kept confidential; provided, however, that such information may be disclosed to those employees or agents of the Recipient who have a need to know such information, or to such other party as may be agreed to by the Disclosing Party in writing, only if each such employee or party is informed by the Recipient of the confidential and proprietary nature of such information and of the confidentiality undertakings of the Recipient contained herein.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient may disclose Confidential Information to anyone, without restriction, at its sole discretion.
perturbation.explanation: Removing all confidentiality obligations violates standard contract principles and can be argued against the covenant of good faith. It also creates a risk of trade secret misappropriation if the information qualifies as a trade secret under Pennsylvania law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under PUTSA, trade secret misappropriation occurs when someone discloses or uses another's trade secret without consent. If the Confidential Information meets the definition of a trade secret and the recipient discloses it without restriction, it could violate PUTSA.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates that the recipient keeps confidential information secret, but the changed text permits the recipient to disclose this information to anyone without any restrictions. The Law Explanation states that under PUTSA, trade secret misappropriation occurs when someone discloses another's trade secret without consent. The Perturbation Explanation supports this by asserting that removing all confidentiality obligations creates a risk of trade secret misappropriation if the information qualifies as a trade secret. The scraped snippets are empty, however, the Law and Perturbation Explanations in conjunction with the removal of confidentiality obligations from the original text create a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient shall be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by its employees or agents.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient shall not be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by its employees or agents.
perturbation.explanation: Removing liability for employee breaches contradicts the principle of respondeat superior and creates an unenforceable clause if an employee breaches the agreement.
perturbation.law_citation: Principles of Agency Law and Respondeat Superior
perturbation.law_explanation: Respondeat superior holds employers liable for the negligent acts of their employees committed within the scope of their employment. This clause attempts to eliminate that vicarious liability, which may be unenforceable depending on the specific circumstances and the nature of the breach.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: respondeat superior | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you respondeat superior Respondeat superior is a legal doctrine, most commonly used in [wex:tort], that holds an employer or principal legally responsible for the wrongful acts of an employee or agent , if such acts occur within the scope of the employment or agency. Typically when respondeat superior is invoked, a plaintiff will look to hold both the employer and the employee liable . As such, a court will generally look to the doctrine of joint and several liability when assigning damages . Jurisdictional Differences There is not a national standard for respondeat superior . Because states create their own standards for the doctrine, different jurisdictions will use different tests to prove respondeat superior . However, most jurisdictions will use 1 of the following 2 tests: Benefits Test When the employee’s social or recreational pursuits on the employer’s premises after hours are endorsed by the express or implied permission of the employer and are conceivably of some benefit to the employer, then the employer is liable for harm resulting from the employee’s actions. Characteristics Test If the employee's action is common enough for that job that the action could be fairly deemed to be characteristic of the job, then the employer will be liable for harm resulting from the employee’s actions. Strict Liability Comparison A court will choose to apply the doctrine of respondeat superior to an employer, regardless of how closely the employer was monitoring the employee. As such, respondeat superior may be compared with strict liability . Exceptions Independent Contractors Respondeat superior applies to employees, but not to independent contractors . The Third Restatement of Torts helps to outline the difference between an employee and an independent contractor for the purpose of respondeat superior . It presents a fairly thorough balancing test : the extent of control that the agent and the principal have agreed the principal may exercise over details of the work whether the agent is engaged in a distinct occupation or business whether the type of work done by the agent is customarily done under a principal’s direction or without supervision the skill required in the agent’s occupation whether the agent or the principal supplies the tools and other instrumentalities required for the work and the place in which to perform it the length of time during which the agent is engaged by a principal whether the agent is paid by the job or by the time worked whether the agent’s work is part of the principal’s regular business whether the principal and the agent believe that they are creating an employment relationship whether the principal is or is not in business. the extent of control that the principal has exercised in practice over the details of the agent’s work." Federal Employees Under the Westfall Act , federal employees will not be held liable for wrongdoings committed during the scope of their employment. Further Reading For more on the doctrine of respondeat superior , please see this Santa Clara Law Review article , this Michigan Law Review article , and this Louisiana Law Review article . Keywords tort law vicarious liability liability Agency AGENCY LAW joint and several liability JOINT-AND-SEVERAL LIABILITY Wex accidents & injuries (tort law) standards of tort liability tort damages wex definitions Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the doctrine of respondeat superior. The original text stated that the recipient 'shall be responsible' for breaches by employees/agents, aligning with the doctrine. The changed text states the recipient 'shall not be responsible', which directly negates the employer's liability for employee actions within the scope of employment. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines respondeat superior as 'a legal doctrine...that holds an employer or principal legally responsible for the wrongful acts of an employee or agent, if such acts occur within the scope of the employment or agency.' The change attempts to eliminate this legally established vicarious liability, creating a clear conflict with the law. This contradiction is direct, enforceable, and removes a core legal obligation.

perturbation.original_text: Each party understands and acknowledges that the Disclosing Party is not making any representation or warranty as to the accuracy or completeness of any Confidential Information furnished by or on behalf of the Disclosing Party (except to the extent and only to such effect as shall be expressly set forth in an executed and delivered definitive agreement between the parties to effect the Stated Purpose (the "Definitive Agreement")). Neither the Disclosing Party, its affiliates nor any of their respective officers, directors, employees or agents shall have any liability to the Recipient or any of its Representatives relating to or arising from the use of the Confidential Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Disclosing Party warrants the accuracy and completeness of all Confidential Information. The Disclosing Party, its affiliates, and their respective officers, directors, employees or agents shall have unlimited liability to the Recipient or any of its Representatives relating to or arising from the use of the Confidential Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Creating unlimited liability with guaranteed accuracy is highly unusual and may conflict with Pennsylvania's limitations on liability for certain types of damages or misrepresentations, particularly if the information is used for investment or business decisions.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania law on negligent misrepresentation and limitations of liability
perturbation.law_citation: Pennsylvania case law on negligent misrepresentation (e.g., Gibbs v. Ernst & Young) and limitations on liability.
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania law, while allowing for contractual limitations of liability, may not enforce clauses that are unconscionable or violate public policy. A complete warranty of accuracy coupled with unlimited liability for *any* use of the information could be deemed unreasonable, especially in complex business contexts. Gibbs v. Ernst & Young touches on standards for liability related to the dissemination of inaccurate information.

perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a significant legal contradiction by introducing an unlimited liability clause coupled with a warranty of accuracy, a departure from typical legal standards. The original contract explicitly denies any warranty regarding the accuracy of confidential information. The altered text reverses this, warranting the accuracy and completeness of the information and imposing unlimited liability for its use. The Law Explanation suggests this could be deemed unreasonable under Pennsylvania law, particularly in complex business contexts, potentially violating public policy, and references Gibbs v. Ernst & Young regarding liability for inaccurate information dissemination. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 doesn't directly address liability limitations, it emphasizes the importance of court oversight and legal processes within Pennsylvania, implicitly supporting the notion that contractual terms must adhere to legal and public policy considerations, which the modified contract arguably fails to do by creating potentially unconscionable terms. This makes the contradiction direct and impactful, warranting a YES.
